sail.FY2023-24: AMBIGUOUS: SAIL FY2022-23 SOx is reported in concentration units only, not comparable to mass-based values

sections.about: SOx emissions measure sulphur oxide emissions, primarily driven by sulphur in fuel, coke, coal and process gases. This is disclosed under BRSR Principle 6, Essential Indicator for air emissions other than GHG emissions. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Across the three-year series, Jindal Stainless was the lowest SOx emitter in all three years: 0.85189 kt in FY2022-23, 3.0721 kt in FY2023-24, and 4.5807 kt in FY2024-25, reflecting the smaller stainless-steel operating footprint and EAF-heavy process route versus larger BF-BOF integrated carbon-steel producers. In FY2024-25, SAIL was second at 18.21 kt, JSW improved to 34.64 kt, and Tata Steel had the highest emissions at 46 kt. Tata’s SOx moved from 51 kt in FY2022-23 to 38 kt in FY2023-24, then worsened to 46 kt in FY2024-25. Tata’s FY2025 BRSR notes that capex includes investments for SOx, NOx and dust reduction, while its air-quality mitigation relies on advanced pollution-control technologies, real-time emissions monitoring, upgraded equipment, new technologies and maintenance.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranked 4th on SOx, with the highest emissions among peers. Tata’s 46 kt was 41.42 kt above Jindal Stainless’s 4.58 kt, approximately 904% above the best performer. Tata’s rank moved from 3rd in FY2022-23 to 4th in FY2023-24 and stayed 4th in FY2024-25. The trajectory is one of persistent underperformance on this metric: absolute SOx improved in FY2023-24 but worsened in FY2024-25, and JSW’s improvement to 34.64 kt in FY2025 means Tata now trails all three peers.
sections.targets: Tata discloses investments and mitigation initiatives for SOx reduction.
sections.comparabilityNotes: SAIL FY2022-23 reports SOx in concentration units (µg/m3), which cannot be converted to mass without stack flow rate data and is excluded from the FY2023 peer comparison. JSW FY2022-23 value is derived from 1.69 kg/tcs times 20.87 million tonnes crude steel production, giving 35.27 kt. Jindal Stainless reports in metric tonnes in the BRSR; values are converted to kilotonnes for peer comparability. SAIL FY2024-25 value is derived from 0.95 kg/tcs times 19.17 million tonnes crude steel production, giving 18.21 kt.

flags.flagNote: SAIL FY2022-23 SOx is reported in concentration units only and cannot be converted to mass. The FY2022-23 to FY2023-24 trend for SAIL cannot be assessed on a like-for-like basis.
